retrieved_docs.title: Small Cell Lung Cancer -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: According to the World Health Organization (WHO) Classification of Tumors, “SCLC is defined by light microscopy as a tumor with cells that have a small size, a round-to-fusiform shape, scant cytoplasm, finely granular nuclear chromatin and absent or inconspicuous nucleoli.” Necrosis may be extensive, and cells manifest a high mitotic rate. SCLCs originate from epithelial cells, and up to 90% will express thyroid transcription factor-1 (TTF1). Epithelial cell markers including cytokeratin can be used to distinguish SCLC from lymphoma and other neuroendocrine tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Histopathology -- Microscopic Features
retrieved_docs.content: The differentiation of retinoblastoma tumors plays a significant role in prognosis. Well-differentiated tumors typically exhibit abundant Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes and Fluerette patterns and are associated with a better prognosis. In contrast, poorly differentiated tumors lack rosettes and display sheets of undifferentiated small round cells. These tumors are characterized by higher proliferative activity and more aggressive clinical behavior. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Brain Tumors -- Histopathology -- Embryonal Tumors
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumors (ATRT) are embryonal tumors characterized by sheets of cells with round, eccentric nuclei, necrosis, and frequent mitoses. Areas composed of small, round, blue cells may also be noted. [27] These tumors are characterized by inactivation of the SMARCB1 and SMARCA4 genes. They have been subtyped into 3 subgroups: ATRT-MYC, ATRT-SHH, and ATRT-TYR. Among these, the ATRT-TYR group has superior overall survival, whereas the ATRT-SHH group has a higher likelihood of metastatic disease and poorer outcomes. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyosarcoma -- Histopathology -- Histologic Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: Characteristic histologic findings of RMS tumors include undifferentiated embryonic skeletal cells. Each histologic subtype can usually be differentiated by cell morphology, though there may be some mixed types. [1] [11] Sheets of medium-sized cells arranged in large clusters separated by fibrovascular septa are commonly seen in alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma. Scattered giant cells are often seen also. Embryonal subtypes typically have small round blue cells and loosely packed rhabdomyoblasts within a myxoid matrix. Spindle cell types are usually arranged in a pseudovascular pattern and are more differentiated than the other subtypes. Pleomorphic subtypes can often be identified due to having undifferentiated cells with a variety of shapes with enlarged hyperchromatic nuclei. [13] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Astrocytoma -- Histopathology -- Specific Tumor Types
retrieved_docs.content: Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma Prognosis is typically good Children and young adults affected Big lipidized cells May mimic malignant tumor

retrieved_docs.title: Omental Tumors -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Desmoplastic small round cell tumor requires immunohistochemistry to differentiate them from other entities such as Ewing Sarcoma. Desmoplastic small round cell tumor demonstrates nests of small round blue cells with scant cytoplasm and small nucleoli within desmoplastic fibrous connective tissue, staining positive for cytokeratin, desmin, vimentin, and neuron-specific enolasein. [26] Omental small cell carcinoma demonstrates small cells with scant eosinophilic cytoplasm, oval or spindle-shaped nuclei with stippled nuclear chromatin, and stains for CD56, cytokeratin, synaptophysin, and neuron-specific enolase. [13] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: CNS Ewing sarcoma family tumor with CIC alteration is densely cellular with small round cells. The tumor has both alveolar and fascicular patterns of growth. It does not have markers of differentiation. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Malignant Pleural Effusion -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Small round cell tumors, including Ewing and rhabdomyosarcoma, are the most common malignancies involving pediatric body fluids and serosal surface involvement. Small round cell tumors reveal a consistent morphology characterized by clusters of small round cells with central nuclei, scant cytoplasm, and frequent small vacuoles. While immunocytochemistry is valuable for accurate diagnosis, additional ancillary studies may be required, especially in hematologic malignancies. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Mediastinal Carcinoid Tumors -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: These tumors are cataloged based on the WHO classification for pulmonary neuroendocrine tumors, in typical and atypical carcinoid, large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma, and small cell carcinoma. [14] Histological classification is based upon the presence or absence of necrosis, mitoses, and large cell or small cell cytology. The nuclear chromatin, in general, is finely granular, while large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma may show prominent nucleoli. Tumor cells may form trabeculae, rosettes, festoons, glands, and solid sheets. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors -- Histopathology -- Histologic Diagnostic Studies
retrieved_docs.content: On gross examination, GISTs typically appear as well-circumscribed, submucosal masses arising from the muscularis propria, often with a smooth, lobulated surface. Cut sections may reveal a tan to gray-white, fleshy appearance with areas of hemorrhage, cystic degeneration, or necrosis in larger or more aggressive tumors. Microscopically, GISTs are categorized into 3 major histologic subtypes: spindle cell (the most common), epithelioid, or mixed types. [14] Spindle cell type GISTs are described as having cells arranged in short fascicles or whirls, whereas epithelioid cell GISTs have cells arranged in a diffuse or nested pattern. Mixed cell-type GISTs incorporate both spindle cell and epithelioid cell histologic patterns. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Cancer -- Pathophysiology -- Small Cell Carcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: Small cell carcinoma (SCLC) is composed of round, oval, or angulated cells, with a small amount of cytoplasm and size roughly that of a resting lymphocyte. No distinct nucleoli are seen. SCLCs are extensively necrotic. They usually stain positive with chromogranin or synaptophysin. The WHO previously classified SCLC into three cell subtypes: oat cell, intermediate cell, and combined cell (SCLC with NSCLC component, squamous, or adenocarcinoma). However, studies have shown that such classification does not have much clinical significance or prognostic value. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Wilms Tumor -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: "Favorable" Histology: Ninety percent of Wilms tumors will demonstrate "favorable" histology, which generally has a better prognosis. Classical histological features of a "favorable" Wilms tumor include a triphasic pattern of blastema, epithelial, and stromal tissues.  The blastema is the most undifferentiated and possibly the most malignant component.  It consists of collections of small, round blue cells with very active mitotic activity and overlapping nuclei.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Histopathology -- Microscopic Features
retrieved_docs.content: Retinoblastoma is composed of poorly differentiated small, round, blue cells that resemble immature retinal cells. These cells have hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, resulting in a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio. Mitoses are frequently observed, reflecting the tumor's high proliferative activity. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebellopontine Angle Cancer -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Meningiomas have a varied histological appearance and are classified into several types based on the predominant histological architecture. Common varieties include meningothelial (syncytial and epithelial cells with poorly defined borders and classic whorls), fibroblastic (firm tumor comprised of spindle cells and undefined cell borders), and transitional (both meningothelial and fibroblastic components). Other less common subtypes include angiomatous, psammomatous, metaplastic, microcystic, secretory and lymphocytic. WHO grading system divides meningiomas into three grades, i.e., grade I to grade III based on histological features, presence of anaplasia, and prognosis. The majority of meningiomas are benign, slow-growing (grade I). [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Medulloblastoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In classic medulloblastoma, sheets of small round cells, possessing a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, are noted. They have a high invasive tendency and possess occasional neuroblastic differentiation. The classic type constitutes approximately 70% of medulloblastomas. Nodules of tumor cells displaying neurocytic differentiation on a collagen-rich matrix characterize the desmoplastic variant; these tumors are less aggressive than the classic variant and account for 15% of medulloblastomas. Large-cell anaplastic medulloblastomas, as the designation suggests, demonstrate features of anaplasia. These features are large tumor cells with abundant cytoplasm, pleomorphic nuclei, and prominent nucleoli. These tumors are typically located in the cerebellar vermis and are highly aggressive, demonstrating high mitotic and apoptotic activity with large areas of necrosis. Consequently, the prognosis is especially poor with short survival times after diagnosis. They constitute approximately 10% of medulloblastomas. [18] The last two variants are rare and make up the remaining 5% of medulloblastomas. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracic Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary NETs are cataloged based on the WHO classification: low-grade (typical) NETs (TCs), intermediate-grade (atypical) NETs (ACs), LCNEC, and SCLC. [22] Histological classification is based upon the presence or absence of necrosis, mitotic activity, and large cell or small cell cytology. The nuclear chromatin, in general, is finely granular, while LCNEC may show prominent nucleoli. Tumor cells may form trabeculae, rosettes, festoons, glands, and solid sheets. [22] TCs and ACs are both classified as well-differentiated. However, AC exhibits more aggressive clinical behavior compared to TC. LCNEC and SCLC represent 2 distinct types of poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas, classified separately. Despite differences in grade and clinical behavior, all pulmonary neuroendocrine neoplasms share key features, including the ability to synthesize neuropeptides and the presence of submicroscopic cytoplasmic dense-core granules. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: cancer; other and unspecified primary and سرطان الغدة الدرقية are both classified as subtypes of ورم صماوي خبيث, which denotes a malignant tumor. This relationship indicates that these specific cancers fall under the broader category of malignant tumors, sharing the characteristic of uncontrolled, invasive cell growth. Recognizing them as forms of ورم صماوي خبيث helps guide diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: common respiratory symptom. Together, these relationships demonstrate how the classification integrates diverse malignant neoplasms, connects specific tumor subtypes to broader malignant categories, identifies anatomical sites of disease extension, and relates morphological descriptors and clinical findings across organ systems, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as abnormal cells of disease and are classified under the category “cancer; other and unspecified primary,” indicating that these cells represent a form of cancer for which the primary site cannot be determined. This classification underscores the diagnostic uncertainty surrounding such tumor cells and highlights the need for further pathological evaluation to clarify their nature and guide appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, benign are not considered a finding of the disease category cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that benign tumor cells do not represent this type of cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: similarly defined as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as the abnormal cells that underlie the disease entity known as bone tumor (ورم العظام).

Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured view of digestive system cancers, especially pancreatic malignancies, their metastatic characteristics, and their placement within a comprehensive neoplasm classification system that spans multiple organ sites and includes both well‑defined and uncertain neoplastic entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2591
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant are identified as abnormal cells of disease within the category “cancer; other and unspecified primary,” and this particular cancer classification is mapped to thymický karcinom (thymic carcinoma).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not considered a finding associated with ovarian cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 838
retrieved_docs.content: a specific instance of secondary malignancy in defined locations. Other entities listed as members of the non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ include سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة (lung carcinoma with non‑small cells) and the various metastatic and primary head‑and‑neck cancer forms described above, illustrating that the SMQ classification aggregates a wide spectrum of malignant neoplasms, both primary and metastatic, across different anatomical sites and histologic types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2645
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, benign are regarded as not a finding of disease, indicating that the presence of benign tumor cells does not constitute a pathological diagnosis. In contrast, the category cancer; other and unspecified primary is classified as a type of malignant tumor (ورم صماوي خبيث), reflecting its nature as a cancerous condition. Recognizing that Tumor cells, benign are not disease findings while cancer; other and unspecified primary represents malignant neoplasia helps clinicians differentiate non‑pathogenic cellular changes from clinically significant cancers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, represent the abnormal cellular component of السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). Their ambiguous nature underscores the diagnostic challenge in distinguishing benign from malignant lesions within the pancreas, influencing treatment decisions and overall patient management.
